data.title: Newmont: Significant Margin Expansion In Q2
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless otherwise noted. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day. UG = Underground.

Q2 Production & Sales

Newmont (NYSE:NEM) released its Q2-25 results this week, reporting quarterly production of ~1.48 million ounces of gold, an 8% decline from the year-ago period. Lower production was related to the divestment of the bulk of its Canadian mines (Porcupine, Eleonore, Musselwhite), Telfer (Australia), CC&V (Colorado) and Akyem (Ghana), as well as higher production at Ahafo (Ghana), Penasquito (Mexico), Cerro Negro (Argentina), Yanacocha (Peru) and Lihir (Papua New Guinea).

Increased production Cerro Negro was related to easy comps on a year-over-year basis given the temporary suspension related to tragic fatalities in the year-ago period, while its large-scale Cadia and Penasquito mines benefited from better than expected grades in Q2. Overall, this has left Newmont sitting at ~51% of its production guidance midpoint of 5.9 million ounces (~3.01 million ounces produced year-to-date), and continuing to be the largest gold producer by a wide margin, as shown below.

On a negative note, even assuming Newmont meets guidance, production per share has taken another meaningful step lower and has continued its persistent downtrend. This is partially related to the difficulty of consistently replacing reserves at a 5.0+ million ounce scale, but also because of less accretive acquisitions than some of its peers. It's also one reason the largest producer in the sector with the highest depletion makes for a poor long-term investment, with NEM still trading at the same price as it was during 1996 despite an 8x move in the gold price in the same period.

While puzzling for a company that produces primarily gold, the 8x move in the gold price is far less meaningful for a business that sees declining production over a 20-year period at the same time as its share count nearly triples from ~400 million shares to just shy of ~1.1 billion shares, explaining much of this underperformance vs. elite per share growers like Agnico Eagle (AEM) and Alamos Gold (AGI), among others.

As for Newmont's sales performance, gold sales came in at ~1.37 million ounces, down from ~1.53 million ounces in the year-ago period. Similar to production, this was related to fewer ounces sold from its large Ontario portfolio (Q1-24: ~206,000 ounces combined) that was sold in addition to other non-core asset sales, as well as fewer ounces sold from Cadia (Australia), Tanami (Australia), and fewer copper tonnes sold in the period (~37,000 vs. ~39,000).

Fortunately, this was made up for by a record realized gold price of $3,320/oz, benefiting from mark to market on provisionally priced sales ($25/oz positive impact), with revenue jumping 21% year-over-year to ~$5.32 billion, with 86% of revenue from gold (Q1-24: 82%). Let's dig into the results a little closer:

Quarterly Production by Mine/Jurisdiction

Starting with Newmont's quarterly gold output by jurisdiction, West Africa and Papua New Guinea dominated with ~24% of total production, with over 10% of production from Mexico. This was followed by ~23% of production from Australia, ~21% from South America, ~16% from the United States and 4% from Canada, down from 17% from Canada in the year-ago period. This resulted in just ~44% of Newmont's production coming from Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions, down 1,200 basis points from ~58% in the year-ago period and closer to 65% in Q2-23.

As for production by mine, over 15% of revenue came from Penasquito (Mexico), and nearly 40% from Tier-3 ranked jurisdictions (Ghana, Papua New Guinea, Mexico), up significantly from the year-ago period.

Moving to individual mine performances, Ahafo produced ~402,0000 ounces in H1-25 at $1,341/oz AISC, up from ~374,000 ounces in H1-24 albeit at much higher costs (H1-24: $1,066/oz AISC). As for its Q2 results, production was ~197,000 ounces at industry-leading AISC of $1,220/oz, up from ~184,000 ounces at $1,123/oz in the year-ago period. This was related to higher mill throughput at similar grades (~2.39 million tonnes processed at 2.73 G/T of gold), but we won't see the same strong performance out of this asset in H2-25 given expected lower grades.

Fortunately, while Ahafo South's grades are set to dip, Ahafo North is bringing up the rear with its expected gold pour by year-end and with ~$770 million spent on this low-cost Ghanian mine to date.

Moving to Brucejack in Canada, production came in at ~91,000 ounces in H1-25 at $2,363/oz AISC, translating to a significant increase in costs with lower production levels. As for Q2-25 performance, performance was just as disappointing with ~50,000 ounces produced at $2,490/oz AISC, down from ~60,000 ounces at $1,929/oz in the year-ago period. Lower output was related to lower grades (5.31 G/T of gold vs. 7.33 G/T of gold) and recoveries which more than offset higher throughput in the period.

As for Penasquito in Mexico, H1-25 production came in at ~271,000 ounces of gold at $1,013/oz AISC, also showing solid cost performance vs. ~109,000 ounces at $1,055/oz in H1-24. Looking at Q2-25 production, we saw gold production of ~148,000 ounces at $944/oz AISC, benefiting from higher grades and recoveries (0.85 G/T of gold) vs. the year-ago period. Unfortunately, Penasquito's gold production will fall in H2-25 with mine sequencing into areas richer in silver and base metals.

Looking at Merian (Suriname), production was ~115,000 ounces at $1,986/oz AISC in H1-25, down from ~135,000 ounces at $1,820/oz in the year-ago period. As for its Q2-25 results, production came in at ~53,000 ounces at $2,074/oz AISC (Q1-24: ~61,000 ounces at $2,170/oz), benefiting from lower sustaining capital spend in the period.

As for Cerro Negro (Argentina), H1-25 production was ~70,000 ounces at $2,936/oz, down from ~100,000 ounces at $1,635/oz AISC. Q2-25 production also came in at extremely high costs ($3,023/oz AISC), with increased throughput (~183,000 tonnes at 7.83 G/T of gold) at lower grades offset by much higher sustaining capital spend in the period. The good news is that sustaining capital is front-end weighted, with better cost performance to come in H2-25 from this high-grade mine.

Moving to Australia, Boddington and Tanami combined for ~441,000 ounces in H1-25 and ~237,000 ounces in Q2-25. This was down from the year-ago period (H1-24: ~478,000 ounces) at significantly higher costs, with Boddington and Tanami's H1-25 AISC coming in at $1,482/oz and $1,680/oz, respectively. At Boddington, Q2-25 grades were lower offset by higher throughput, contributing to lower production in the period. As for Tanami, grades also fell (4.24 G/T of gold vs. 4.91 G/T of gold), with AISC spiking to $1,698/oz in Q2-25, impacted by higher and contracted services costs for UG mining.

As for Cadia, its large gold-copper porphyry with a 25+ year mine life, production came in at ~207,000 ounces at $1,144/oz AISC in H1-25, down from ~239,000 ounces at $1,028/oz AISC in H1-24. Meanwhile, Q2-25 output also slid to ~104,000 ounces at $1,109/oz AISC (Q1-24: ~117,000 ounces at $1,064/oz AISC), impacted by lower grades (0.52 vs. 0.65 G/T of gold). It's worth noting that while Cadia's AISC came in nicely in H1-25, this asset is tracking miles behind its capex budget for 2025, with just ~$273 million of a planned ~$820 million spent in H1-25, suggesting heavy capex in H2-25 and a significant impact to mine-site free cash flow.

This is especially the case for sustaining capital, which is sitting at a mere ~27.8% of planned full-year spend, which combined with lower grades in H2-25 will result in much higher operating costs for this asset, a drag on Newmont's cost performance in Q3 and Q4.

Finally, Newmont's Lihir Mine, a massive mining complex sitting within the Luise Caldera of the Luise Volcano on the east coast of Lihir Island. H1-25 production was ~324,000 ounces at $1,450/oz AISC, an improvement from ~322,000 ounces in H1-24, albeit at higher operating costs. As for Q2-25 performance, production was ~160,000 ounces at $1,563/oz AISC, with significantly higher costs than the year-ago period despite higher output (~2.17 million tonnes at 2.66 G/T of gold and higher recoveries). Unfortunately, grades at this major asset are expected to be lower in H2-25 as well and this will be coupled with catch-up on sustaining capital spend that's also tracking behind annual guidance. Let's take a look at the financial results:

Financial Results

Digging into Newmont's financial results, revenue came in at ~$5.32 billion (+21% year-over-year), operating cash flow came in at ~$2.38 billion (+71% year-over-year) and free cash flow came in well ahead of my expectations at ~$1.71 billion (+188% year-over-year). The increase in free cash flow was largely related to record gold prices, slightly better than expected production, but mostly significantly lower capex (down 16% year-over-year) that completely diverged from the company's original guidance that H2-25 would mark peak sustaining capital and H1-25 would see the lion's share of development capital.

And while this is great news for the Q2-25 results and its net debt position that slid to just ~$1.4 billion net of significant share repurchases, it puts a burden on the Q3-25 results and H2-25 results that now need to see significant catch-up on spending across several areas.

For starters, sustaining capital is sitting at just ~42.8% of planned full-year spend with ~$771 million of a planned $1.8 billion spent in Q1-25. Meanwhile, development capital is sitting at ~48.6% of planned spend, with higher capex to come here as well. As for exploration/advanced project spending, it's also tracking miles behind guidance at ~36.9% of full-year guidance at ~$193 million of a planned $525 million. Finally, reclamation and remediation saw only ~$280 million spent in H1-25 of a planned $800 million, with the bulk of this related to water treatment facilities at Yanacocha.

So, Newmont will have to contend with much higher capex in H2-25 at the same time as grades are dropping at several of its key lower-cost assets (Lihir, Ahafo South, Cadia, Penasquito), setting up a much higher cost H2-25 on deck.

Costs & Margins

As for cost performance, Newmont's Q2-25 AISC came in at $1,593/oz, well below expectations and estimates. This helped to push AISC margins to $1,727/oz (~52.0%) on the back of a record gold price, up from $779/oz (~33.2%) in the year-ago period. In terms of year-to-date cost performance, H1-25 AISC are sitting at $1,623/oz, tracking better than full-year guidance. However, this is a direct result of a massive shift in planned spending into the second half of the year vs. prior expectations for sustaining capital to peak in H2-25. Hence, I do not see a path where Newmont delivers at/below its $1,630/oz AISC, and I would expect a material increase in AISC in Q3-25 and Q4-25 towards $1,700/oz or higher.

To better illustrate this point, we can see planned full-year sustaining capital spend (red bar) at some of its larger assets vs. what has been spent year-to-date (yellow bars). As we can see, several assets are tracking well behind plans and the same is true of development capital where Newmont has spent just ~41% of planned growth capital at Cadia, and ~37% of planned growth capital spend at Red Chris. This doesn't mean that Newmont isn't going to put up solid results in Q3-25 and Q4-25 and that's certainly possible if gold prices continue to hang out above $3,300/oz.

However, investors should be aware that the primary reason for this beat on free cash flow and strong headline numbers was the decision to push capex into the second half of this year. This includes planned optimization of road access and pit design at Lihir moved to H2-25, capex to address historical underinvestment in tailings at Cadia moved to H2-25, and planned spend at Brucejack/Red Chris, as well as increase spend on ventilation work at Tanami. Simultaneously, we will see a significant increase in reclamation spending at Yanacocha.

Recent Developments

Red Chris Incident & Proposed Block Cave

As for the most recent incident at Red Chris, we can breathe a sigh of relief given that the three contractors for Hy-Tech Drilling that were trapped underground for over 60 hours after two fall of ground incidents at the mine were rescued yesterday. Fortunately, the workers were able to get to a refugee chamber equipped for 16 people with fresh air, food, and water for an extended period, with a remote scoop able to remove a 20-30 meter long and 7-8 eight meter high pile of debris to help clear the path for getting out of the mine.

As for the future of Red Chris, this incident at the non-producing Red Chris UG is where Newmont hopes to build a massive block cave. If the company's amendment to its EA Certificate is approved, this would involve a plant expansion to 15MTPA (11 MTPA currently), installing a material handling system and underground infrastructure and significant underground development as well as an expansion of the electrical substation. The construction of Red Chris Block Cave is expected to take at least three years with ~3,000 direct full-time positions during construction or nearly 1% of BC's construction workforce.

As for concerns related to the expansion, there is some concern about job losses due to the change in mining method to a more specialized and highly-skilled block cave, as well as potential job losses from increased automation. Other concerns stem from road traffic during the construction period. Newmont's preference would be to train and transition existing open-pit employees at Red Chris to operate the block cave, suggesting less of an impact in terms of job losses. As for the production profile, MB1 ("Macro Block 1") production could come in as high as ~340,000 ounces of gold and ~90,000 tonnes of copper (assuming 15MTPA expansion is approved) in its first six years, morphing into a ~600,000 ounce operation on a gold-equivalent basis or ~420,000 GEOs to Newmont at lower costs.

While this is a massive asset that will certainly make a meaningful contribution at attractive margins, I would be surprised to see commercial production from MB1 before 2032 given the timeline for construction (3+ years) and need for full permits for this major amendment to head underground with a large footprint block cave.

Share Repurchases

As for share repurchases, Newmont significantly stepped up its share repurchases in Q2-25, purchasing ~19.3 million shares at an average price of ~$52.50, with ~2.47 million shares repurchased at $58.87 subsequent to quarter-end. This has resulted in it completing over 90% of its authorized $3.0 billion share repurchase program with another $3.0 billion share repurchase program authorized. Notably, this recent share repurchase program was funded entirely with non-core asset sales with more proceeds to come from recent equity divestments, with its share count now down to ~1.1 billion shares, a step in the right direction after the significant dilution stemming from its past two major acquisitions that did little to increase gold production.

In fact, its share count is up over 100% since year-end 2017, while gold production is up ~10% from ~5.3 million ounces to an expected ~5.9 million ounces this year and 5.6 - 5.7 million ounces in 2026.

Valuation

Based on ~1.1 billion shares and a share price of $66.00, Newmont trades at a market cap of ~$72.6 billion and an enterprise value of ~$74.0 billion. This makes it the highest market cap name in the sector by a wide margin, with it now trading at over $12,500/oz per ounce of gold production on an EV basis, an expensive valuation relative to peers when adjusted for its inferior amount of production coming from Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions (Q2-25: ~44%) and its inferior track record of per share growth. Obviously, stocks do not trade solely on valuations, and this doesn't mean that NEM can't head higher from current levels. However, I see limited margin of safety at current levels and I don't see any reason to chase the stock here above $66.00 at ~15x FY2026 EV/FCF estimates ($3,100/oz gold price assumption).

So, what's a fair value for the stock?

Using what I believe to be generous multiples of 1.3x P/NAV (7% discount rate) and 10x FY2025 cash flow estimates, I see an updated fair value for Newmont of $69.80. I believe these multiples are generous given Newmont's position as a consistent per share decliner with less than half of gold production from Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions and its inferior growth profile to some of its peers in the major producer space. This points to limited upside from current levels and while it's certainly possible that could exceed this fair value estimate, I continue to see far more attractive bets elsewhere in the sector.

This is especially true given that Newmont's deferred capex has created difficult comps vs. the first half of this year, setting up a weaker free cash flow profile sequentially (H2-25 vs. H1-25) unless the gold price heads to new highs.

Summary

Newmont put up solid headline results in Q2-25, benefiting from slightly stronger than planned production with better grades at Cadia and Penasquito, a record gold price and front-end weighted production at many of its larger and lower-cost assets. However, the main culprit for the beat was deferring significant capex to the back half of this year at the same time as it will simultaneously be spending at a higher rate on exploration/advance development spend and reclamation spend. The result is much more difficult comps on H2-25 vs. H1-25 results and while production is tracking against guidance, the steep decline in production per share and most per share metrics should not be ignored.

To summarize, I don't see any way to justify chasing the stock here above $66.20 and I would view any rallies above $70.20 before September as an opportunity to book partial profits into strength assuming I were still long the stock.

data.title: First Hawaiian, Inc. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by First Hawaiian, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Rollins: Growth Engine Firing In All Cylinders
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Rollins (NYSE:ROL) previously with a buy rating as everything was moving in the right direction: pricing, execution, retention, and margin expansion. My conviction has strengthened after looking at this quarter’s result. The commercial strategy is scaling well, pricing remains firm despite macro pressure, and it continues to demonstrate operating leverage. With strong momentum into 3Q and several long-term cost and growth initiatives underway, I believe ROL is well positioned to continue growing.

2Q25 earnings

ROL delivered another strong quarter, reinforcing my confidence in the business. Revenue grew 12.1% y/y to ~$1 billion, with organic growth of 7.3%, which is impressive itself given the tough weather backdrop in May. All major service lines delivered double-digit growth. Termite & Ancillary saw 13.9% y/y, followed by Residential with 11.6% and Commercial with 11.4%. The momentum following into 3Q25 has been extremely encouraging too. Management called out several daily sales records and a strong backlog moving into July, and if momentum holds, I remain confident that there is a real chance ROL lands toward the high end of its 7–8% annual organic growth target.

Margins were slightly softer, with adj. EBITDA margin down 50 bps y/y to 23.1%, but this was due to a 70 bps drag from a one-off reserve related to legacy auto claims. Excluding that, incremental margins were around 25%, which is more in line with ROL’s historical performance. Lastly, adj. EPS grew 11.1% y/y to $0.30.

Commercial Strategy Continues To Work Well

In my last update, I pointed to early signs that ROL Orkin commercial realignment was gaining traction. 2Q25 results confirmed that things have progressed more than expected. Commercial organic growth accelerated sequentially by 100 bps to 8.4%, and that recurring revenue growth has accelerated to “double digits,” a step up from the “closer to 10% y/y” remark previously. This is solid evidence that the commercial strategy is working.

I expect this growth momentum to continue as ROL is apparently stepping up focus in this front, as seen from the promotion of Scott Weaver to the newly created COO role for Orkin Commercial. This is a very important signal, as I believe creating a C-suite-level role for a specific business unit formalizes this initiative as a core long-term growth driver. Management specifically called this an “elevated role,” which, in my view, implies greater decision-making authority and day-to-day accountability. From an execution standpoint, this creates a clear chain of command. A divisional COO’s job is to translate strategy into results (i.e., making sure the sales force, service teams, and support functions are fully aligned to scale the business). I also think Scott is the best guy for this role because he has been part of this entire commercial initiative over the past few years, so he knows the ins and outs.

To sum this up, while having a new COO role is a very high-level qualitative data point, I believe it marks a key turning point, in that this strategy will now be running on full steam, with a faster decision-making process and better resource allocations. All of this should sustain the current growth momentum.

Discretionary product with strong pricing Power Reinforce Defensive Moat

ROL pricing power and premium positioning were very apparent in 2Q25. The Termite & Ancillary segment posted 10.3% organic growth, and while this was a deceleration from 11.1% in 1Q25, I argue that 2Q25’s 10.3% is more impressive because it was against a tougher comp base—1Q24 saw 9.3% organic y/y vs. 2Q24 with 11.1% organic y/y. On a two-year stack basis, organic growth actually accelerated by 100 bps. And the more impressive thing here is that June growth came in several hundred basis points above the quarterly average. This suggests momentum has accelerated even further, which could point to another quarter of >20% two-year stack organic growth despite 3Q24’s 13.7% organic y/y.

Contrasting to the macro environment, where discretionary spending is under pressure, I think this just shows how resilient ROL’s business model is. Termite protection isn’t something consumers easily cut back on, and this provides more justification for ROL to flex its pricing muscle. We know this is true because pricing is currently running at 3–4% (the same as 1Q25), with no noticeable increase in churn this quarter.

The point here is that a sticky discretionary service with high pricing power provides another layer of confidence that ROL can hit its 7–8% organic growth target.

Operational Leverage Evident Despite Growth Investments

A key bullish development this quarter is the strong operating leverage even while ROL is investing for growth. Despite staffing up for peak season, total revenue managed to grow 12.1% y/y in 2Q25, while “Employee expenses” within the cost of services line item grew at a slower rate of 11.3% y/y, and this essentially drove a 30 bps improvement in gross margin (the COGS line fell from 30.1% to 29.8%). The way I see it, this is the result of ROL’s technician retention efforts—better retention reduces the need for constant backfilling with less efficient new hires. While selling and marketing expenses grew slightly ahead of revenue, which is kind of expected during a peak season to capture demand, they should come down during the non-peak season. The important thing is that the labor efficiency gains meant that ROL’s long-term margin structure has improved.

ROL’s cost structure should get better ahead as management has implemented a “value creation program”—essentially an initiative aimed at reviewing all cost categories, from back-office automation to working capital. We don’t know the impact yet, but any cost savings here goes straight to improving incremental margins.

Just to reiterate, ROL incremental adj. EBITDA margin is still running at 25% on a like-for-like basis, which means there is still ample room for overall margin to expand (FY24 adj. EBITDA margin was 22.8%).

Valuation

My current model has more granularity in the revenue growth drivers. Previously, I only assumed ROL to deliver organic growth, but looking at the recent results where M&A has been an active driver (2Q25 inorganic growth contributed almost 500 bps), I think it makes sense to incorporate M&A in my growth assumptions. My bottom-up build is as follows: 3.5% from pricing, 4% from volume (in line with 2Q25 organic growth), and 2% from M&A (a discount from recent inorganic contributions). Together, revenue should grow at 9.5% for the next few years. For margins, I kept my assumption the same, that it should continue to expand since incremental margin is at 25%. As I expect higher growth, I think a higher forward EBITDA multiple is warranted, which I think the market has priced in already—ROL now trades at 31x forward EBITDA (vs. my 30x assumption previously). All this translates to a new share price target of $69.

Risk

The primary risk is near-term margin pressure. Legacy auto claims are hard to predict, and as seen from 2Q25, they impacted margins by 70 bps. Also, if growth investments in marketing and commercial do not deliver expected returns, profitability could be negatively impacted. While ROL has proven its products are resilient, a significant economic recession could still force consumers to delay spending on pest control services.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for ROL. The 2Q25 results confirmed that the business is moving in the direction I expect it to. Commercial growth has inflected, pricing power remains intact, and cost discipline is showing up in the margins. Management’s focus on stepping up its commercial push and rolling out a value creation program adds even more upside to the long-term story. With my new price target of $69, I see 19% upside.

data.title: Booz Allen Hamilton Q1: Weak Government Procurement Environment, But Undervalued
data.description:
I assigned a Strong Buy rating to Booz Allen Hamilton Holding Corporation (NYSE:BAH) in November 2024, highlighting its strong market position in AI and cybersecurity. The company is facing significant growth challenges due to a weak government procurement environment. However, I believe the sluggish growth is temporary and its stock price is significantly undervalued. I reiterate a Strong Buy rating with a fair value of $145 per share.

Booz Allen Hamilton released its Q1 FY26 results on July 25th before the market open, delivering 0.6% revenue decline and 2.4% growth in adj. net income, as detailed in the table below. It is evident that its business has been decelerating in recent quarters due to the weak government procurement environment.

During the earnings call, the management acknowledged the soft IT consulting demand from government agencies due to the Trump administration’s push for cost savings and terminations of some discretionary IT consulting projects. As these government agencies are in the process of reviewing all consulting projects, the management anticipates the company’s civil business will return to growth in the second half of FY26. In other words, investors should be prepared for another quarter of weak growth in Q2 FY26.

With the civil business being reset, Booz Allen Hamilton has initiated several efforts to save the margin, including reducing staff in the civil market and optimizing its cost structure, as noted during the call. In addition, the company has been shifting its contracts to outcome-based terms to align with the government's cost-saving requirements. Overall, I think the management has done the right things to restructure its civil market to adapt to the evolving market demand. Having said that, the near-term growth challenges will persist in the upcoming quarters, in my view.

As depicted in the slide below, the company remains committed to a balanced capital allocation strategy including dividends, share buybacks and investments in new technology. As discussed in my previous article, cybersecurity, data analytics and AI are key growth areas in the civil consulting market. As such, Booz Allen Hamilton has to invest in these new technologies to capture future growth. In addition, the company targets a 2.5x net debt leverage. I think the company has a strong balance sheet, considering the anticipated $900-$1,000 million in FCF for FY26.

Due to the weak government consulting market, Booz Allen Hamilton is guiding for 0%-4% total revenue growth with 11% adj. EBITDA margin for FY26, as detailed in the slide below:

I anticipate Booz Allen Hamilton’s business will remain weak in Q2 FY26, then start to recover in the second half of FY26. Apparently, most IT consulting companies are working with the government agencies to terminate some discretionary consulting projects and transition some contracts to outcome-based structures. It is quite possible that the overall market will normalize in the second half of FY26, then return to growth. As such, I forecast Booz Allen Hamilton will deliver 3% total revenue growth, assuming 1% growth from acquisitions and 2% organic revenue growth for FY26. From FY27 onwards, I anticipate the company will deliver 7% revenue growth, comprising 6% organic growth and 1% growth from M&A. I assume the company will allocate 0.8% of revenue towards M&A, aligning with its historical average.

As Booz Allen Hamilton has been restructuring its civil business, I anticipate its reported operating margin will decline to 9.9% in FY26, assuming $70 million in additional costs from restructuring activities. From FY27 onwards, I project the company will deliver 10bps margin expansion, primarily driven by the operating leverage of SG&A expenses.

The DCF can be summarized as follows:

I calculate the FCFF as follows:

The WACC is calculated to be 9.9% assuming: risk-free 4.2%; beta 1.2; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $14 billion; debt $3.4 billion; tax rate 24%. I set the terminal growth rate to be 4.5%, aligned with the overall government spending growth. Discounting all the future FCFF and adjusting the net cash, the fair value is calculated to be $145 per share, as per my estimates. The current stock price is only trading at around 15x forward FCF, a quite low multiple in my view.

Key Risk

As discussed previously, the company is more likely to deliver another weak result in Q2 FY26. Its stock price might remain weak until the end-market starts to normalize in the second half of FY26. In addition, the U.S. government agencies might take a much longer time to review all consulting projects, potentially causing further delays in new consulting projects. In that case, Booz Allen Hamilton might not see its civil business recover in the second half of FY26, and its full-year growth guidance would be optimistic.

Conclusion

While Booz Allen Hamilton is facing strong growth challenges in the civil market, I view these headwinds as temporary, and its technology is highly relevant in the government’s IT modernization initiatives. I reiterate a Strong Buy rating with a fair value of $145 per share.

data.title: Virtus Investment Partners, Inc. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Virtus Investment Partners, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: GOVZ: Attractive Only When Fiscal Discipline Returns
data.description:
Thesis

Treasuries have been in the news a lot as of late. While Fed Funds and money market yields have come down after the Fed started cutting rates in September 2024, long dated treasury bonds have not seen their yields move significantly lower. In today's article we are going to explore the iShares 25+ Year Treasury STRIPS Bond ETF (BATS:GOVZ), an exchange traded fund that contains very long dated treasuries. We are going to look at the fund composition, its structuring and risk drivers, and asses the macro context for the name.

What does GOVZ hold? Treasury STRIPs

Well let us look at the fund objective first, which details what it aims to do:

The iShares 25+ Year Treasury STRIPS Bond ETF seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of the principal payments of U.S. Treasury bonds (specifically principal “STRIPS”, also known as “Separate Trading of Registered Interest and Principal Securities”) with remaining maturities of at least 25 years.

STRIPs are just treasury securities where the interest and principal cash-flows have been separated or 'stripped'. STRIPs retain the same characteristics as regular treasuries (i.e. guaranteed payments, no credit risk), but they just have different duration profiles. To that end we can see the very high duration of the ETF:

  • Duration: 26.6 years
  • Weighted average maturity: 27.3 years

The fund contains only 22 individual holdings, and the full Excel can be downloaded here. Investors need to remember that in essence GOVZ is a long dated treasuries fund, and thus acts as a proxy for the long end of the treasury curve. A long duration simply means the possibility of gains is maximized if long rates move lower. For example, a 100 bps shift in the 20- and 30- year treasury curves will result in a +26.6% gain for GOVZ. The fund has a much higher duration than the better known iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT).

What have we seen in the yield curve?

Since the start of the year we have seen an interesting development in the yield curve:

In the above graph the red line represents the current yield curve, while the blue line is the curve as of the start of the year. We can see the front end of the curve staying fairly flat (as no new rate cuts got enacted), all while the belly of the curve (1y to 5y) moved much lower in anticipation or future cuts. Also very interesting to note that the long end (i.e. 10 years and higher) generally moved wider, especially the 20- and 30-year nodes.

The market is basically telling us that while the Fed controls the front end and will be able to affect tenors up to 5 years, the long end belongs to market participants, and they do not like the fiscal picture which is emerging out of the United States.

The market owns the long end and it does not like deficits

The Fed's ability to influence the long end of the curve is very limited. Let us take a look:

We have plotted effective Fed Funds versus the 30-year rate and done the correlation factor between the two. We can see the correlation is low historically, with swings from positive to negative. There are times where Fed Funds is low, yet the long end of the curve moves up, and times when Fed Funds moves much lower, but the 30-year rate stays put.

Investors need to understand that future Fed cuts will not necessarily translate into lower long rate, quite the opposite if the market feels inflation is not slayed and lower Fed Funds can fuel a comeback in inflationary pressures. The long end will only come down if it feels deflationary forces at work, with no signs of a re-acceleration in inflation down the line.

The second item to consider for the market relates to a ballooning budget deficit:

The U.S. is running very high fiscal deficits despite a long stretch of economic growth and prosperity. As we can see from the above graph, fiscal stimulus tends to happen around economic crises, when the government needs to help the economy. We saw this during the Great Financial Crisis of '08-'09 and the Covid pandemic. Large deficits need to be financed from somewhere (money is not free), thus the market moves the long end of the curve to account for the uncertainty regarding fiscal imbalances. The market can push economies into true hurricanes, as we saw with the Greek debt crisis:

The Greek Debt Crisis was a severe financial crisis that began in 2009 when Greece faced significant challenges in repaying its government debt or refinancing it due to high debt levels, structural economic weaknesses, and a loss of investor confidence. Greece's public debt reached unsustainable levels, peaking at around 180% of GDP by 2011. This was driven by years of excessive government spending, tax evasion, and inaccurate fiscal reporting.

2009-2010: Yields spiked as investor confidence collapsed. By April 2010, 10-year bond yields exceeded 7%, signaling Greece’s inability to borrow affordably in markets. After the first bailout, yields temporarily eased but remained volatile.

2011: Yields surged dramatically, reaching 28% in late 2011 as fears of default grew. The PSI debt restructuring and second bailout were direct responses to this crisis.

The long end of the U.S. yield curve is therefore a great barometer for the U.S. fiscal health. If the market feels the country is not on a sustainable fiscal path, it will push long dated yields higher. If the market is confident regarding government policies, it will move yields lower. As of December 2024, the U.S. government debt was reported at 124.0% of nominal GDP, up from 123.1% in the previous quarter, according to CEIC. While not catastrophic, the path has been higher in the past decade, and the government needs to show it is able to move this ratio lower.

Peers

There are several ETFs which investors use to gain duration in the treasuries market. Let us look at them and their duration profiles:

  • 7.3 years duration: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF)
  • 16 years duration: ICE U.S. Treasury 20+ Year Bond Index (TLT)
  • 23 years duration: Vanguard Extended Duration Treasury ETF (EDV)

All of these ETFs are composed of treasuries or treasury STRIPs, thus contain no credit risk. They are all exposed to the moves in yields for their respective nodes in the curve, with the higher the profit and loss sensitivity as duration increases. GOVZ has the highest duration of the cohort, thus represents a very volatile bet on long yields.

Let us look at some analytics for the fund as well:

  • AUM: $0.27 bil
  • Yield: 5%
  • Standard deviation (3Y): 24%
  • Net expense ratio: 0.1%

Conclusion

GOVZ is a fixed income exchange traded fund. The name focuses on very long dated treasury STRIPs, and has a 26.6 years duration. The name does not contain any credit risk, only rates sensitivity via its composition. The long end of the U.S yield curve is controlled by the market, and has little correlation to Fed Funds. The market is currently concerned about high fiscal deficits and the possibility of a return of inflationary pressures. With a high standard deviation (3-year) of 24%, the ETF is only to be bought once investors have a clear picture regarding the government's fiscal policies and impact on the deficit.

data.title: OceanFirst Financial: Key Metrics Are Soft
data.description:
OceanFirst Financial Corp. (NASDAQ:OCFC) is a smaller New Jersey-based regional bank that we continue to follow. We follow many regional banks because they give us a pulse-check on local economies. This year, during this critical Q2 earnings season, we are seeing continued strong performance in these names as a whole. The stock is offering a still near 5% dividend yield, yet the key metric performance of the bank is giving us some pause. We are certainly cautious about the bank's loan portfolio and asset quality. The company is seeing a bump in operating expenses, and mixed performance on the key metrics we follow. Further, OceanFirst does a lot of commercial real estate loans, as opposed to other banks we cover that do more consumer/residential loans. This adds some performance risk. In this column, we check back in on the key metrics of the bank from the just-reported Q2 earnings.

OceanFirst Financial's Q2 headline earnings a double-line miss

Folks this was a mixed quarter at best. There was a double-line beat on both revenue and earnings. Revenue was a miss by $6.9 million, up 0.8% from last year, and EPS was a $0.05 miss. L

Loans and deposits

Total loans increased by $67.0 million to $10.19 billion, from $10.12 billion at the start of the year. That is positive. On top of this, the loan pipeline increased by $648.1 million to $954.8 million, from $306.7 million. This was mostly due to an increase in commercial loans of $593.3 million. Deposits increased by $166.1 million to $10.23 billion, from $10.07 billion at the start of the year. There was a nice bump in time deposits. Time deposits increased to $2.30 billion, from $2.08 billion. The loan-to-deposit ratio was 99.5%, meaning there are more deposits than loans.

Credit reserves and book value

Provisions were $3.0 million, though this is down from $5.4 million in the sequential quarter. While provisions weigh on earnings, there were down some, but it still weighed. Book value dropped here. Book value per common share decreased from $29.27 in Q1 to $28.64 to start the year, while tangible book value increased to $19.34, as compared to $19.16 to start the quarter. On the valuation front, this is a bank that is trading at a discount to tangible book, which you do not see too often, given the current price of $17.89. This reflects, in our opinion, risk being baked into the price given the sensitivity to the economy with the commercial loans. However, we can see why this is attractive to investors. Risk is why there is a discount.

Return metrics and asset quality mixed

Return metrics were mixed, but efficiency improved. This comes as the net interest margin widened in the quarter to 2.91%, up a basis point from 2.90% in Q1. Asset quality overall was mixed. Net charge-offs were $2.2 million this quarter versus charge-offs of $0.64 million in the linked quarter. Non-performing loans, those at high risk of default, decreased to $33.5 million from $37.0 million at the start of quarter and represented 0.33% and 0.37% of total loans, respectively. The allowance for loan credit losses as a percentage of total non-performing loans increased to 297% as compared to 213% in Q4. On a positive note, the level of 30 to 89 days delinquent loans decreased to $12.2 million from $33.6 million, mostly related to commercial loans. These are mixed trends here folks.

The efficiency ratio also softened from a year ago. The efficiency ratio was 71.93%. In the sequential Q1 it was 65.67%, and in the year ago Q2 it was 62.86%. The return metrics were poor. The return on average assets fell 6 basis points from Q1's 0.62% to 0.56%, and is down heavily from a year ago The return on average tangible common equity cratered from 7.47% in Q1, to 6.36%.

Final thoughts

We still think the discount to the tangible book value is attractive if you are considering buying, and you get a near 5% yield. But there is risk here on the commercial side of the business, and we are seeing negative trends in return metrics. Asset quality is mixed. This report suggests the bank, relative to so many others we cover, shows the company is struggling in the current environment. When it comes to the key metrics that we follow for banks that we have seen, it is relatively soft. We would not be buyers and give shares a hold rating.

data.title: The Long-Term Appeal Of Lundin Mining
data.description:
Notice: All amounts in dollars are Canadian dollars.

Given Lundin Mining's (OTCPK:LUNMF) partially Swedish roots, I've often gotten requests to look at the company. However, I don't often look into international mining businesses, as their volatility makes them (typically) too unpredictable to really work with for my approach. I can work with broader businesses that work on both the smelting and selling, such as Norsk Hydro (OTCQX:NHYDY), as well as Boliden (OTCPK:BDNNY). I have also previously covered and invested in businesses like Franco Nevada (FNV) - but not Lundin, until now at least. I figured with the recent trends in copper, as well as tariffs, it was a good time to give readers a general overview of the company, as well as what I think of the appeal and investability.

So we'll use recent results and market trends to put a value on the operations and assets of Lundin Mining. We'll look at its financials, its yields, and its estimates to see what it can be worth. We'll consider peers to see if any of them are arguably better or qualitatively safer investments (this does happen quite a bit). Furthermore, we'll look at overall risks.

Lundin Mining, while operated and owned in part by Swedes and much of its history is Swedish (with originally Swedish assets), is headquartered in Canada under the TSX symbol LUN - if I were to invest in the company, it would be through this symbol, despite the fact that the company has a native ticker on the Swedish stock market.

Let's see what we have here.

Lundin Mining - Upside from Mining at the right price, but plenty of volatility

The company goes back to the early 90s as a diamond mine investment company, founded by Adolf Lundin. Early on, it invested in a company exploiting northern Swedish resources, and it cooperated with the aforementioned Boliden to work several Swedish zinc and copper mines. The company was listed on NASDAQ Stockholm in -03, and on the TSE in -04.

Over its history, the company has owned several assets, including the historical Swedish Zinkgruvan, a zinc-lead-silver mine, which it bought from Rio Tinto (RIO), and over the years, it has owned several mines both by itself and through joint ventures with companies like Rio and BHP (BHP).

The company is 15% owned by the Lundin family. The Lundin family is well-known in Sweden, and unfortunately, not necessarily in a positive way. While the mining company is generally exempt from the worst of the scandals, the family's oil company is not, with family members currently on trial for war crimes in Sweden committed by the family's oil company in South Sudan in the late 1990s involving the exploitation of natural resources. The Company is now known as Lundin Energy, and is private, and the assets in question were sold to a company called Talisman Energy over 23 years ago. The company and the family members have consistently denied any wrongdoing.

In terms of the mining company that we're covering here, I would say that while there are some historical battles, such as environmental violations and fines, these are not in any way atypical of a mine operator and company of this kind. I see no issues here or any significant financial risk in investing in the company.

The company's current operations are found primarily in copper mining, with mining also in zinc, gold, and nickel. The company's current primary producing assets are copper mines in Atacama, Candelaria, and Caserones, as well as Chapada in Brazil, a copper mine in the US (Eagle), mining assets in Portugal, and the aforementioned Zinkgruvan in Sweden (but the company is cycling assets, more on that below). So as you can see, the company has international operations. It's US-based assets with copper also quite effectively insulate it in part from some of the tariffs levied against copper, given that they produce locally.

Furthermore, the company owns a 50% interest with the aforementioned BHP in projects in both Argentina in Chile.

The company's production has been very solid over the past few years, with 150k tonnes of zinc and 2M oz. of silver alone, even early on. And in fact, 2024 was a superb, record year for the company, with its 30-year mark achieved.

The company has one of the strongest growth profiles from any mining peer - and this is in part due to successful M&A's and executing on attractive, inorganic growth.

And the company's growth targets are lofty. For copper, what I would consider the keystone resource for the company's results and growth, the company is targeting 30-40 kt production growth until 2030, with a 10% incremental increase in production. However, the company is also targeting growth in other areas, such as gold and silver, and is focusing, among other areas, on its Vicuna district in South America, one of the primary geographies currently operated by the company.

The company's production is more concentrated than you'd think. Candelaria, for instance, which the company owns at 80%, contributes over 40% to the company's quarterly revenues - and it also has a remaining mine life of over 20 years at this time. The company has furthermore insulated itself from some operational risks by outsourcing the underground mining of the mine (but the company is actually about to change this to lower costs), as well as being the progress of some pit optimization. Mining cost for the asset, as a result of the company's continued investments and optimization, is down significantly.

As mentioned above, one of the company's near-term goals is so-called "insourcing," which I too have seen in sectors I work in (being a consultant), where companies are trying to shift strategies in order to reduce reliance on comparatively costly contractors/consultants. Typical arguments for this include lower cost, efficiencies, better internal processes, and more control - but in exchange, obviously, for higher risk.

With the company's important US-based asset, the Eagle mine, the company owns this 100%. However, the main drawback is that this mine is at the end of its rope, with a mine life of only about 5 years remaining at this time.

It's not a bad asset, but one that Lundin needs to plan to "cycle out."

1Q25 results, the latest we have here, were relatively solid. It shows us first, just how central copper is to the company, as well as how central Candelaria and Caserones is to the company.

For the time being, and given what macro challenges seem to present themselves, the company managed both good volumes and good prices. There were some inventory delays resulting in higher production costs, but there were also factors such as higher gold prices and good FX that benefitted the company. Following the company's many asset rotations and shifts to reduce its leverage, Lundin Mining now has a net debt of 1x to EBITDA on an adjusted basis, with a net debt in total of $1.44M, and with $1.5B of available liquidity. As such, the company's liquidity and debt are well in hand, and are in fact better than most miners comparable to Lundin.

With the latest mines to be found and planned, I would say Lundin is a world-class copper mine operator that seems likely to, over the long term, grow significantly in both top-line and profitability. It's in fact a company I would invest in, provided I could get it at a good price.

The primary risks to Lundin from an operational and high-level perspective are, first and foremost, environmental risks, but also stability and forecast risks. Lundin Mining, like most mining companies, is incredibly difficult to forecast. Despite its large market cap and significant operational size with international exposure, the company misses EPS negatively over 60% of the time on a. historical basis, and misses positively 27%+ of the time. That means that any estimate that's given to Lundin needs to be taken with more than a grain of salt. Even the record 2024E results, on an adjusted EPS basis, came in 26% less than was estimated, and you can see examples of the volatility below.

This brings us to valuation for the company, which I would estimate at the following level.

Valuation for Lundin Mining - Looking at a long-term upside, but too expensive now.

Lundin Mining typically trades at an 11-19x P/E - a relatively wide range of multiples, not entirely unexpected for a volatile mining company with the sort of forecast accuracy I showed you above. It's equally important to showcase that over time, the company has mostly underperformed the SPY, especially on longer-term timeframes like the 15-20 year average (7.86% 20-year annualized RoR, next to 9.21% of SPY). I typically invest long-term in companies that outperform - companies with volatility make a better case for a value-oriented investment, rather than a long-term one. And Lundin, given its volatility with beats of over 250% at times, and misses of over 84% at other times, makes for what I view as an excellent value-oriented investment, at the right price.

Unfortunately, the current estimated annualized rate of return at what I would consider a historically conservative multiple works out to become negative, at least at a 14-15x forward P/E ratio, which is at the level of the 3-5 year average of 13-15x P/E.

But we can estimate the company at close to 19x P/E, and still come out negative, especially without the company's yield (only 0.8%, but still). So, from that perspective, despite a growth of around 3% per year until 2027E, the company is set to deliver relatively poor results, with some companies at far better positions to deliver alpha. Estimating the company at any P/E higher than 19x is also not something I am prepared to do - in fact, I don't believe it's worth 19x given its yield and unrated status. At a CAD price of roughly $14/share, I believe the company is significantly overvalued next to alternatives.

One alternative is Boliden. The company currently trades at an 11x P/E, it far more often beats result estimates (72%+ of the time), and based on a 5-year normalized P/E of 10x, it currently implies a fair value of 400 SEK, with an upside of 13.7% per year. Not market beating, but better than Lundin.

Analysts for Lundin put the company at a mix of "Buy", "Overweight," and "Hold," based on 23 analysts following it. Price targets range from a low of $13.64 to a high of $19.37, with an average of $16.5/share. That means that most analysts believe the company, for some reason, is worth a lot more than 19-20x forward P/E, or that earnings will be significantly higher than expected and forecasted.

I personally cannot reconcile that possibility with the company's forecast accuracy. I believe this accuracy is not irrelevant, and thus I use the 17-19x P/E normalized as a fair value estimate, coming to about $15.5/share at most. This, in turn, implies a price target, for a 15% annualized upside, of less than $11.5/share. If you believe this to be too low for the company to trade at, I refer you to the fact that back in April, 3 months ago, the company did trade at that level briefly.

I now have a price alert for Lundin Mining and would be interested at that level, both for the longer term as well as taking advantage of obvious short-term trading trends.

Risks and considerations for the company are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

My "HOLD" rating for Lundin Mining has the risk of the company outperforming averages, or the stock outperforming as it has in the past. The company's asset sales from an international perspective have given the company stability that could result in higher returns and results. If this were to happen, the price target needs to go up. The continued JV's could also increase the company's counter-cyclical resilience, and this is hard to estimate for.

However, I believe the company is better suited for a "HOLD" here.

Thesis

  • Lundin Mining is a complex mining company with a solid long-term upside, owned 15%+ by the billionaire Lundin Family out of Sweden. The company operates mining assets across the world, with a focus on South America, which represents more than 80% of revenue and profit.
  • I believe the company is likely to be one of the major copper players on a forward basis, and I believe, at the right value, the company is worth investing in. Based on company estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a fair value target of $15.5/share, which in turn comes to a $11.5/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15.5% annualized.
  • I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "HOLD" rating and wait until we see a better valuation.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this and a valuation target of $11.5/share, where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "HOLD" rating as of July 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NFLY: Great Option To Boost Profits On Netflix
data.description:
Introduction

The YieldMax NFLX Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:NFLY) is a fund that seeks to provide consistent income, on a monthly basis, by selling and writing calls on the underlying security, Netflix, Inc. (NFLX). This fund tries to provide investors with a consistent yield, while retaining capped upside on gains for NFLX. This ETF’s main objective is income, and its secondary objective is to benefit from share appreciation in NFLX. Below are some key facts from the prospectus.

  • Expense Ratio: 0.99%.

  • AUM: $169.5M.

  • Volume: 413K.

Its top holdings are shown in the image below - as you can see, cash in the form of US Treasuries and then various derivative exposures on NFLX.

In the section below, we will review recent relevant news and headlines for NFLX and see if investors should be open to gaining exposure to NFLX through this ETF.

Recent News & Trends

Earnings

NFLX recently reported earnings, and despite a beat on both top and bottom lines, the shares slipped following the announcement. To sum up the highlights: EPS came in at $7.19 versus $7.08 consensus, and revenue was $11.08B versus $11.07B estimates. Overall revenue grew 16% compared to the previous quarter, and the firm updated its full-year guidance to be between $44.8B and $45.2B, up from a range of $43.5B to $44.5B. These all look fairly solid upfront. Again, for the second quarter in a row, NFLX has determined it would be in their best interest not to release an update on subscription data, which leaves something to be desired, but it looks like the firm has been able to grow nicely this past quarter. Furthermore, net cash from operations also increased, which is always something that we like to see as it shows how well a company’s core business is doing at producing cash, and it looks like NFLX did very well here. Their $2.4B cash generated from operations was up 84% from the prior-year period, and free cash flow guidance was also increased to $8-$8.5B. All of these look strong upfront, and we believe that this is mainly driven by 2 factors: 1) their continued clamp down on sharing accounts, which makes more individuals purchase their own subscription, and 2) their ongoing investment into original content creation. Shows that are in the pipeline, such as Stranger Things, have a devout fanbase, and NFLX has only stirred the interest more for their upcoming next season with their trailer that was recently released. We view their slight downturn since earnings as a possible catalyst to enter a position in the company, as fundamentally speaking, their reports look healthy.

Use of AI in their content creation

NFLX has been reported to use AI in creating content, and we think that despite being a bit of a controversial issue in filmmaking, this can be good from an investor’s perspective. As AI is used to generate backgrounds and improve special effects, the overall cost of production should in theory decrease over time as the AI models are trained to be better and better over time at enhancing video quality and output. We do not expect nor believe that a majority of a video production will be AI-generated (we hope that this is not the case) but we certainly believe that there can be time/effort, and therefore cost, savings generated from using AI to touch up raw video footage. Per the CEO, Ted Sarandos, there were efficiencies of up to 10x faster production by using AI to enhance visual effects and optimize workflows. In the future, viewers may find it difficult to determine what has been AI-generated and what has not been, but from an investment standpoint, it may soon be undeniable how significant the cost savings and productivity enhancements AI can generate for the company. This represents a positive benefit for the company in the future.

Market share remains strong and their original content is a powerhouse

Despite the growth in streaming competitors, the data shows that NFLX has taken top positioning amongst its peers in the US video streaming on demand market for original content in movies. See the graph below that highlights the top players.

NFLX is clearly beating even Disney+ which has a massive trove of original content under the Disney umbrella.

NFLX is also in the lead when it comes to original content, TV shows as well. Despite other players catching up a bit in this segment, their lead is still definitive - see below.

We believe that NFLX will continue to do well in original content, and per a report, the firm is set to spend $18B in developing original content this year alone. This figure is also set to rise, as the CFO states in the report. We view this as very bullish for NFLX shares in the long run and feel that with their lead in market share, continuing to create great content will only push them further. This is another positive sign for investors looking to gain exposure to NFLX.

Covered Call Strategy

NFLY’s strategy is to own NFLX stock (either directly or synthetically via options) and then buy/sell calls to generate income. The fund will employ one of two strategies at any given time: selling covered calls or selling a covered call spread. We will explore each of these strategies below.

Covered Calls

The covered call strategy involves owning shares of a stock and then selling call options against that holding at a strike price that is higher than the current price. Let’s use the following scenario to explore this strategy:

  • We buy 100 shares of NFLX at $100 a share.

  • We sell a NFLX call option at a strike price of $110 for a premium of $300.

The chart below shows your potential returns depending on the price of NFLX.

The blue line represents your returns if you did not sell the option and the red line represents your returns if you did sell the option. We see that the return with options increases with NFLX’s share price, but after it hits $110 (the strike price of the option), returns are fixed and do not increase. On the flipside, the options strategy is always higher than the strategy without options when looking at any price below $113. This is because we gained $300 by selling the option.

Overall, this strategy is good if NFLX increases a little, but not beyond $110. If NFLX decreases in value, your losses are lower because you made money on selling the call option.

Now let’s look at the covered call spread scenario. A covered call spread is one in which an investor sells a call option and then buys a call option at a higher strike price. NFLY “writes” covered call spreads, meaning that they actually do the opposite - they buy a call option and then sell a call option at a higher strike price.

Scenario:

  • Buy 100 shares of NFLX at $100 per share.

  • Buy a $110 strike call option for $300.

  • Sell a $120 strike call option for $150.

In this scenario, if NFLX goes up by enough, then you will make more money than just buying the equity. However, if NFLX decreases in value or does not increase enough, you will be worse off by selling the options. The amount by which you are worse off in this scenario is $150 because you spent $300 to buy one call and made $150 to sell the other for a total expenditure of $150. This is a good strategy to employ if you are expecting NFLX to increase significantly and want to boost your gains.

Overall, each strategy is best in different scenarios:

  • Selling Covered calls: When you expect NFLX to increase mildly or possibly decrease.

  • Selling a covered call spread: When you expect NFLX to increase significantly.

Risks

There are many risks when it comes to buying NFLY, and they are all outlined in detail in the summary prospectus including risks related to derivatives, and using synthetic positions. One risk we want to highlight is the fund’s management. We explored the 2 types of strategies the fund uses, but in order to make a profit from these strategies you need to know 1) which one to use at a given period of time and 2) which calls to buy and/or sell. Both of these decisions are dependent on a good prediction of how the price of NFLX will move. The good news is that as long as NFLX increases in value you will always either make a profit or suffer a relatively small loss. However, there is also the consideration of opportunity cost. In the covered call scenario we discussed, if NFLX increases to $120, you would make $1,300 in profit. But if you had not sold any calls you would make $2,000 in profit meaning that you left $700 on the table. Overall, the managers of NFLY have a difficult job of predicting NFLX’s price movement and making the correct decisions and it is a very real risk that they may not.

Conclusion

We are very bullish on NFLX as it has strong earnings growth, clever AI capabilities to reduce costs, and is focusing investments on original content. The two options strategies the fund utilizes can help boost income from NFLX especially if you are bullish on NFLX’s overall prospects. There is, of course, the risk of the options strategy actually resulting in lower profits, but considering the most recent distribution rate of 32.71%, we see that the fund so far has more than delivered.

data.title: Flagstar Financial, Inc. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Flagstar Financial, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.
